rookie.title: The Midnight Pact
rookie.flavor: Lord Pemberton lies dead in his study, a glass of brandy still warm beside him. The storm outside mirrors the chaos within these walls, where old secrets and newer betrayals collide.
rookie.emptyState: The manor keeps its secrets. Tonight, one proved fatal.

items.name: Margaret Pemberton
items.detail: Lord Pemberton's devoted wife of twenty years.

items.name: Charles Whitmore
items.detail: The family solicitor handling Pemberton's financial affairs.

items.name: Eleanor Hawthorne
items.detail: Pemberton's estranged sister who arrived unexpectedly tonight.

items.name: Thomas Fletcher
items.detail: The loyal butler who has served the family faithfully.

items.name: Poisoned Brandy
items.detail: Lord Pemberton's evening drink, laced with deadly toxin.

items.name: Letter Opener
items.detail: An ornate silver blade from Pemberton's desk drawer.

items.name: Fireplace Poker
items.detail: Heavy iron implement found beside the hearth.

items.name: Revolver
items.detail: Pemberton's service weapon from his military days.

items.name: Library Study
items.detail: Pemberton's private sanctuary where he was found dead.

items.name: Grand Staircase
items.detail: The ornate centerpiece connecting all floors of the manor.

items.name: Servants' Kitchen
items.detail: Below stairs where meals and drinks are prepared.

items.name: Drawing Room
items.detail: The main reception area where guests were entertained.

clues.heading: Butler's Testimony
clues.text: Thomas Fletcher reports serving Lord Pemberton his usual brandy at precisely 10:30 PM. He swears the decanter was untouched when he retrieved it from the locked cabinet.
clues.alone: The butler's account establishes the timeline but raises questions about who had access to the brandy afterward.

clues.heading: Heated Arguments
clues.text: Eleanor Hawthorne was heard shouting at her brother about 'twenty years of lies' around 11 PM. Servants report she seemed devastated rather than angry.
clues.alone: Eleanor's confrontation with her brother suggests family tensions, but her emotional state seems more grief than rage.

clues.heading: Financial Records
clues.text: Charles Whitmore had been quietly embezzling from Lord Pemberton's accounts for months. He was seen nervously pacing the drawing room all evening.
clues.alone: The solicitor's theft provides a clear motive, and his agitated behavior suggests guilt about something.

clues.heading: Kitchen Discovery
clues.text: A vial of rat poison was found hidden in Margaret's sewing basket in the servants' kitchen. She claimed complete ignorance of how it got there.
clues.alone: The poison's location directly implicates Lady Pemberton, though her surprise seemed genuine to witnesses.

clues.heading: Sister's Alibi
clues.text: Eleanor can prove she was writing letters in the drawing room when her brother died - Margaret saw her there and even brought her tea.
clues.alone: Eleanor's whereabouts are confirmed by Lady Pemberton herself, clearing her of the actual murder timing.

clues.heading: Solicitor's Panic
clues.text: Whitmore was seen fleeing toward the stables immediately after the body was discovered. His horse was already saddled, as if he'd prepared for a quick escape.
clues.alone: The solicitor's immediate flight suggests premeditation, but could equally indicate panic over his embezzlement being discovered.

rookie.epilogue: Margaret Pemberton had discovered her husband's affair with Eleanor's daughter through intercepted letters. Twenty years of devotion crumbled into bitter betrayal, driving her to slip poison into his evening brandy. She planted evidence against both Charles and Eleanor, knowing their motives would make them perfect suspects. The loving wife became the perfect killer, hidden behind decades of apparent loyalty. Justice will be swift, but the manor's wounds will linger far longer.

clues.heading: Butler's Testimony
clues.text: Thomas Fletcher reports serving Lord Pemberton his usual brandy at precisely 10:30 PM. He swears the decanter was untouched when he retrieved it from the locked cabinet.
clues.alone: The butler's account establishes the timeline but raises questions about who had access to the brandy afterward.

clues.heading: Doctor's Assessment
clues.text: Dr. Morrison insists the poison was administered through injection, not ingestion, based on the lack of stomach irritation. He points to a tiny puncture wound on Pemberton's neck.
clues.alone: The family physician's medical expertise seems unquestionable, yet his conclusion contradicts other evidence about the brandy.

clues.heading: Financial Records
clues.text: Charles Whitmore had been quietly embezzling from Lord Pemberton's accounts for months. He was seen nervously pacing the drawing room between 10:30-11:00 PM.
clues.alone: The solicitor's theft provides a clear motive, and his presence during the murder window makes him highly suspicious.

clues.heading: Kitchen Discovery
clues.text: A vial of rat poison was found hidden in Margaret's sewing basket in the servants' kitchen. She claimed complete ignorance and can prove she was in the drawing room from 10:30-11:00 PM.
clues.alone: While the poison implicates Lady Pemberton, her solid alibi during the murder window creates a puzzling contradiction.

clues.heading: Brandy Analysis
clues.text: Laboratory tests confirm the brandy glass contained lethal amounts of the same rat poison found in Margaret's basket. No injection marks were found upon closer examination.
clues.alone: The scientific evidence directly contradicts Dr. Morrison's assessment, proving the poison was indeed in the brandy.

clues.heading: Timeline Contradiction
clues.text: Eleanor witnessed Charles in deep conversation with Pemberton in the study at 10:45 PM, fifteen minutes after Fletcher claims to have served the fatal brandy.
clues.alone: This sighting places the solicitor with the victim during the critical window, but raises questions about when the poison was actually administered.

detective.epilogue: Margaret Pemberton had discovered her husband's affair through intercepted letters and spent days planning her revenge. She poisoned the brandy decanter before Fletcher retrieved it, knowing her husband's rigid evening routine. Her alibi was carefully constructed using Eleanor as an unwitting witness while the poison did its work. Dr. Morrison's incorrect assessment was an honest mistake, misled by what he thought was a needle mark. The devoted wife's twenty-year marriage had become her perfect cover for the ultimate betrayal.

items.name: Inheritance Greed
items.detail: Desire to claim estate wealth through elimination.

clues.heading: Butler's Testimony
clues.text: Thomas Fletcher reports serving Lord Pemberton his usual brandy at precisely 10:30 PM. He swears the decanter was untouched when he retrieved it from the locked cabinet earlier that evening.
clues.alone: The butler's account establishes the timeline, but the timing suggests the poison was added to the decanter before service.

clues.heading: Doctor's Assessment
clues.text: Dr. Morrison insists the poison was administered through injection, not ingestion, claiming to see a tiny puncture wound on Pemberton's neck. His previous patient died from a similar 'injection' last month.
clues.alone: The physician's assessment seems compromised by his own malpractice history, possibly projecting his guilt onto this case.

clues.heading: Financial Desperation
clues.text: Charles Whitmore had been embezzling for months and owed massive gambling debts to dangerous creditors. He was seen arguing with Pemberton about 'final payments' around 10:15 PM.
clues.alone: The solicitor's financial crimes and confrontation with the victim create compelling evidence for murder driven by desperation.

clues.heading: Love Letters Discovery
clues.text: Hidden in Margaret's jewelry box were passionate letters between Pemberton and Victoria Ashford, Eleanor's daughter. The final letter spoke of ending their affair and Margaret's growing suspicions.
clues.alone: The correspondence reveals the devastating betrayal that would drive a devoted wife to murder after twenty years of marriage.

clues.heading: Brandy Analysis
clues.text: Laboratory tests confirm the brandy contained lethal amounts of rat poison, administered at least an hour before consumption. No injection marks were found despite Dr. Morrison's claims.
clues.alone: The scientific evidence proves Morrison wrong and reveals premeditation, as the poisoning occurred well before serving time.

clues.heading: The Perfect Frame
clues.text: Margaret had planted evidence against multiple suspects: poison in her own basket to appear framed, forged documents suggesting Eleanor's financial motives, and false witness accounts of Charles's threats.
clues.alone: The elaborate deception reveals a calculated mind that planned not just murder, but the systematic misdirection of suspicion.

inspector.epilogue: Margaret Pemberton's twenty-year marriage crumbled when she discovered her husband's affair with Victoria Ashford, Eleanor's daughter. The betrayal cut deeper because it involved family, turning her devotion into murderous rage. She spent days crafting the perfect murder, poisoning the brandy decanter during her regular household duties and creating an intricate web of false evidence to frame others. Her intimate knowledge of everyone's secrets and schedules made her the invisible killer. The loving wife's final act was the cruelest betrayal of all, destroying not just her husband but the entire family she once cherished. Justice awaited, but the manor would never recover from the poisonous legacy of misplaced trust.
